content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="frequens_vester_67_1"> , <gloss>the sight of you in full assembly</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hic_locus_67_2">, the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Rostra</foreign>. The scanty remains of the <foreign xml:lang="lat">rostra</foreign> may be seen at the left of the Temple of Concord. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_agendum_67_2"> , <gloss>for public business</gloss>, i.e. among the many duties of a magistrate there is none more dignified (<foreign xml:lang="lat"> amplissimus</foreign>) than this of addressing the whole people in a political assembly; <foreign xml:lang="lat">agere cum populo</foreign> was the technical expression for transacting business in the <foreign xml:lang="lat">comitia</foreign> or a <foreign xml:lang="lat">contio</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ornatissimus_67_3"> , <gloss>honorable</gloss> (of private glory as an orator). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Quirites_67_3"> , <gloss>fellow-citizens</gloss>: the name by which the Romans were addressed when acting in a civil capacity. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hoc_aditu_67_3"> , <gloss>this avenue</gloss> (i.e. addressing the people on political questions). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="optimo_cuique_67_4">, i.e. to such as the presiding magistrate would permit, for only these had a right to speak in a <foreign xml:lang="lat">contio</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="rationes_67_5"> , <gloss>plan</gloss>: the plural indicates the details of the plan, i.e. the particular considerations that determine a general course of conduct. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_68_1"> (temporal), <gloss>while</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 546">546</ref> (323, 2); B. 288, I, b; G. 585; H. 600, ii, I (521, 2); H.-B. 524. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="auctoritatem_68_2">: the act of speaking in a <foreign xml:lang="lat">contio</foreign> indicated that the speaker was a proper person to advise the people, and hence it would confer <foreign xml:lang="lat">auctoritas</foreign> ( <gloss> weight</gloss>, <gloss> prestige</gloss>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="attingere_68_2"> , <gloss>aspire to</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="perfectum_ingenio_68_3"> , <gloss>perfected by force of intellect</gloss>, i.e. the fruit of fully developed mental power. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="elaboratum_68_4"> , <gloss>carefully wrought</gloss> (such, therefore, as needed more practice than youth could give). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="amicorum_temporibus_68_5"> , <gloss>exigencies of my friends</gloss>. A Roman lawyer was not regarded as doing a service for hire, but was expected to defend his friends gratuitously. He was, indeed, prohibited from receiving pay; but, though no bargain was made, the obliged party was expected to give a liberal present, in some form or other, to his <foreign xml:lang="lat">patronus</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="neque_et_68_5">: here the first clause is virtually concessive; we may render <gloss>while . . . at the same time</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="caste_68_7"> , <gloss>with clean hands</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="integre_68_7"> (next line), <gloss>in good faith</gloss> (toward the client). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="judicio_68_8">, i.e. their action in electing him. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fructum_68_8">, i.e. the several grades of office he had already filled: he was now <foreign xml:lang="lat">praetor</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dilationem_68_9"> , <gloss>adjournment</gloss> There were many things which could break up an assembly and put off the business, especially unfavorable auguries, the announcement of which was a favorite device of politicians. If an election was thus interrupted by adjournment, the votes already taken were null and void and the whole proceeding had to be gone through with again. The <foreign xml:lang="lat">comitia</foreign> at which Cicero was chosen <foreign xml:lang="lat">praetor</foreign> were twice adjourned in this way, so that there were three meetings before the election was complete. At each of these Cicero was the first (<foreign xml:lang="lat">primus</foreign>) of the eight praetors to secure a majority, and hence he was <gloss>thrice declared elected</gloss> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">ter renuntiatus sum</foreign>). <foreign xml:lang="lat">primus</foreign> does not here imply a superiority in rank, for the eight praetors were regarded as colleagues and they determined their functions by lot. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_aliis_68_11">, etc., i.e. this action of the voters showed that they approved his course of life, and was a suggestion to others how to attain similar honors. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nunc_68_12">, opposed to the time referred to in sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 1">1</ref> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_agendum_68_14">, for <gloss>speaking</gloss> (cf. note on 1.2, above). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="forensi_usu_68_15">: the courts were held in the Forum. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quoque_68_18">, i.e. to forensic as well as to military or political activity. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="atque_68_19"> (the strongest of the <gloss>and's</gloss>), <gloss>and further</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illud_68_19"> (nom.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="laetandum_68_19">: the construction <foreign xml:lang="lat">illud laetor</foreign> changed to the passive; § <ref n="AG 390">390</ref>, c (238, b); cf. B. 176, 2; G. 333, I, N.1; H. 405 (371, iii); H.-B. 397, 2. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mihi_68_20">, following <foreign xml:lang="lat">insolita</foreign>. <pb n="332"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ratione_68_21"> , <gloss> manner</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="oratio_68_22"> , <gloss> language</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="orationis_68_22"> (1.24), <gloss>argument</gloss> (abstract from <foreign xml:lang="lat">oro</foreign>, in its original sense of <gloss>to speak</gloss>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="virtute_68_23"> , <gloss>good qualities</gloss> generally. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="atque_68_27"> (cf. note on 1.19, above), <gloss>and now</gloss> (to come to the point). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="inde_68_27"> , <gloss>from that point</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vectigalibus_sociis_68_29"> , <gloss>tributaries and allies</gloss> (of the latter some were tributary and others not). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="relictus_68_30">, i.e. before the contest was fully decided. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="lacessitus_68_30"> (next line), <gloss>am assailed</gloss>, not seriously attacked. By using these words Cicero artfully prepares for the assertion which he is about to make of the need of an energetic commander. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Asiam_68_31">, i.e. the province of this name, occupying the western half of Asia Minor and bordering on the dominions of Mithridates. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="equitibus_68_32">, etc.: keep the emphasis by changing the construction: <gloss>Roman <foreign xml:lang="lat">equites</foreign> are daily receiving</gloss>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quorum_occupatae_68_33"> , <gloss>whose large properties, invested in managing your revenues, are endangered</gloss>. The revenues were farmed out to <foreign xml:lang="lat">societates</foreign> (companies) of <foreign xml:lang="lat">publicani</foreign>, who were members of the equestrian order (see sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 14">14</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="necessitudine_69_2">: Cicero was of an equestrian family. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Bithyniae_69_4">: this territory had been bequeathed to the Roman republic by Nicomedes III, <date when="-0074">B.C. 74</date>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ariobarzanis_69_5">: king of Cappadocia, which had been overrun by Mithridates. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Lucullum_69_7">: Lucullus was related to both branches of the family of Metellus, and had married Clodia, sister of the notorious Publius Clodius. It was chiefly this mischievous demagogue, who was serving as one of his officers, that stirred up the dissensions and mutinies which robbed Lucullus of the fruits of his victories. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="discedere_69_7"> , <gloss>is on the point of withdrawing</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="huic_successerit_69_7"> , <gloss>his successor</gloss>, Glabrio. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_paratum_69_8"> , <gloss>not adequately furnished</gloss>—an understatement: Glabrio had shown himself thoroughly incompetent, but Cicero was on good terms with him. This was the Glabrio who had presided over the court in the case of Verres. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="socus_69_9">, i.e. Asiatics. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="civibus_69_9">, Romans engaged in business in Asia. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="imperatorem_69_10"> (in pred. appos. with <foreign xml:lang="lat">unum</foreign>), <gloss>as commander</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="agitur_69_17"> , <gloss>is at stake</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="certissima_69_21"> : <gloss>the surest</gloss> because Asia was the richest and most fruitful of all the provinces; hence the price paid by the <foreign xml:lang="lat">publicani</foreign> for the privilege of farming its taxes was always certain to be large. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quibus_amissis_69_22">: equiv. to a fut. <foreign xml:lang="lat">protasis</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ornamenta_69_22"> , <gloss> ornaments</gloss>, i.e. "all that exalts and embellishes civilized life." </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="a_vobis_70_1">: the abl. with <foreign xml:lang="lat">a</foreign> is used instead of the dat. of agent because there is another dat. dependent on <foreign xml:lang="lat"> consulendum</foreign>; § <ref n="AG 374">374</ref>, N.1 (232, N.); B. 189,1, a; G. 355, R.; H. 431, I (388, N.); H-B. 373, I, a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="civis_Romanos_70_8">, etc.: this massacre (<date when="-0088">B.C. 88</date>), in which 80,000 persons perished, was intended by Mithridates as a step toward the entire expulsion of the Romans from Asia. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="regnat_70_11">: for tense, see § <ref n="AG 466">466</ref>(276, a); B. 259, 4; G. 230; H. 533 (467, 2); H-B. 485. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="etenim_70_14"> , <gloss> for</gloss> (you will notice). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="triumphavit_de_70_17">: not <gloss>triumphed over</gloss>, but <gloss>celebrated a triumph for a victory over</gloss>. The word is repeated in emphatic antithesis to the clause <foreign xml:lang="lat">sed</foreign> . . . <foreign xml:lang="lat">regnaret</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="regnaret_70_19"> , <gloss>was still a king</gloss> (i.e. in possession of his kingdom). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="verum_tamen_70_19"> , <gloss>but still</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_egerunt_70_20"> , <gloss>for what they have done</gloss>: by a Latin idiom quod is here equivalent to <foreign xml:lang="lat">propter id quod</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="res_publica_70_22"> , <gloss>the public: interest</gloss> Sulla had hastened to make an unsatisfactory peace, that he might return and restore order in Italy, which was in the power of the Marian faction. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="autem_70_23"> , <gloss> now</gloss> (in contrast to the action of the Roman generals). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="reliquum_70_23"> , <gloss>that followed</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Bosporanis_70_27"> , <gloss>the people of Bosporus</gloss>, a flourishing Grecian state, embracing the Crimea and adjoining lands. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_duces_70_29">, i.e. Sertorius and his comrades. Sertorius was the ablest general of the Marian faction in the Civil Wars. After the victory of Sulla, and the complete overthrow of his own party elsewhere, he continued to hold Spain, where he attempted to found a new republic, entering into alliance with Mithridates and other enemies of Rome. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="gereretur_71_1"> (for fut. indic.): subj. of integral part. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="de_imperio_71_2"> , <gloss>for supremacy</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="alterius_altera_71_3">, 1.6, below. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="firmamenti_71_4"> , <gloss>outward support</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="roboris_71_4"> , <gloss>internal strength</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Cn_Pompei_71_5">: Pompey and Metellus Pius conducted the war against Sertorius from <date when="-0077">B.C. 77</date> till <date when="-0072">B.C. 72</date> without being able to subdue him. In 72 Sertorius was assassinated by his lieutenant Perperna, whom Pompey had no difficulty in defeating. Cicero, it will be observed, suppresses these details, preferring to give Pompey credit, in general terms, for putting an end to "the danger from Sertorius." </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_parte_71_6">, i.e. in the East. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="felicitati_71_8">: observe the chiastic order of the ideas, — <foreign xml:lang="lat"> felicitati</foreign> <foreign xml:lang="lat">virtuti</foreign> ; <foreign xml:lang="lat">culpae, fortunae</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="haec_extrema_71_8"> (an intentional euphemism), <gloss>these late disasters</gloss>, <pb n="335"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tribuenda_71_9"> , <gloss> attributable</gloss>. In fact the ill success of Lucullus was in great part due to the machinations of politicians at Rome; he was not properly supported by the home government. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mercatoribus_71_18">, etc.: abl. abs. expressing cause. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="appellati_71_19"> , <gloss> addressed</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="superbius_71_19"> , <gloss>too haughtily</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="legatum_71_21">, etc.: M'. Aquilius, the person referred to, had in fact forfeited all claim to the inviolability of an ambassador by actually taking command of an army against Mithridates. He was taken prisoner and put to death (<date when="-0088">B.C. 88</date>). Aquilius had done service to the state by suppressing the Servile War in Sicily. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="videte_ne_71_26">: the Latin expresses in the form of a purpose clause ("see to it lest," etc.) what we should put in the form of an indir. quest. ("see whether it be not," etc.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_71_27"> , <gloss> as</gloss>, correl. with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sic_71_27"> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illis_71_27">, i.e. your ancestors. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_posse_71_29">: subject of sit. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_71_30"> a regular formula of transition, <gloss>again</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_71_30"> , <gloss> that:</gloss> § <ref n="AG 572">572</ref>, b (333, b); G. 542; H. 588, 3 (540, iv, N.); H.-B. 552, 1. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="periculum_discrimen_71_30"> , <gloss>a dangerous crisis</gloss>: the former word signifying the <gloss> trial</gloss>; the latter the <gloss>decision</gloss>. (See Introd., p. xlv.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ariobarzanes_72_1">: king of Cappadocia. It was the designs of Mithridates upon this kingdom that first brought him into collision with Rome. (Fig. 20 is from a fine bust of some unknown Cappadocian.) <pb n="336"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="certum_72_6"> , <gloss>a particular</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_72_6">: causal. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sine_periculo_72_7">, i.e. by offending Lucullus and Glabrio. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="propter_72_10"> , <gloss>at hand</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quo_72_10">: abl. of degree of difference with <foreign xml:lang="lat">aegrius</foreign> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adventa_ipso_72_11"> , <gloss>by his mere coining</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="maritimum_72_11">, i.e. the war against the pirates, which Pompey had just finished with great glory. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ceterarum_provinciarum_72_14">, i.e. those assigned to Pompey by the Gabinian Law, which gave him power over the entire Mediterranean and the coasts fifty miles inland. This would not include the province of Bithynia, nor the greater part of Asia. The Manilian Law extended his power over the entire East. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quorum_commendetis_72_15">: § <ref n="AG 535">535</ref>,f(320,f); B. 282,3; G. 631,1; H. 591, 7 (503, ii, 2); H.-B. 513, 3. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="etiam_defendant_72_17">: subj. of integral part. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_multum_72_19">, etc.: the expression was hardly too strong for the general type of provincial governors. cf. "The Plunder of Syracuse," (<ref n="Cic. Ver. 2.4.42">Verr. 2.4.42</ref>, <ref n="Cic. Ver. 2.4.46">Verr. 2.4.46</ref>) where Cicero contrasts the moderation of Marcellus in time of war with the rapacity of Verres in time of peace.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="propter_socios_72_23"> (emphat.): these wars have a place in the argument solely on account of their <gloss>motive</gloss>. The events referred to are the following: Antiochus the Great, king of Syria, was defeated by Scipio Asiaticus at Magnesia, <date when="-0190">B.C. 190</date>. Philip V, king of Macedonia, was defeated by Flamininus, at Cynoscephalae, <date when="-0197">B.C. 197</date>. The Aetolians had helped Rome against Philip, and then joined Antiochus against her; they were obliged to submit after the battle of Magnesia. Carthage had been forced into a third war in <date when="-0149">B.C. 149</date>, and was taken and destroyed by Scipio Aemilianus in <date when="-0146">B.C. 146</date>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="agatur_72_28">, etc., <gloss>it is a question of your richest revenues</gloss>. The province of Asia, like Sicily, paid as a tax the tenth of all products (<foreign xml:lang="lat"> decumae</foreign>). The collection of this was farmed out by the censors to companies of <foreign xml:lang="lat">publicani</foreign> belonging to the equestrian order. All other provinces regularly paid a <foreign xml:lang="lat"> stipendium</foreign>, or fixed tax, which they raised themselves. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tanta_72_29"> , <gloss>only so great</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eis_72_29">, abl. with <foreign xml:lang="lat">contenti</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="via_contenti_72_29">, i.e. they will hardly pay the costs of their own defence. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Asia_72_30">: this description of Asia Minor is no longer true, for bad government and bad cultivation have exhausted its natural wealth. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pastionis_72_32"> , <gloss>pasture land</gloss>, let to publicans, who paid a tax called <foreign xml:lang="lat">scriptura</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="exportantur_72_32">: the <foreign xml:lang="lat">portoria</foreign> were tolls and customs duties paid upon goods both exported and imported; the rate was 2 1/2, or (in Sicily) 5 per cent <foreign xml:lang="lat">ad valorem</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pecuaria_73_8">, etc.: cf. the summary of the resources of Asia, p.72, ll.30-32. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="exercent_73_14"> ,<gloss> manage</gloss>, refers to the <foreign xml:lang="lat">societates publicanorum</foreign>, who took contracts for collecting the revenues; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="exigunt_73_14"> ,<gloss> collect</gloss>, refers to the agents and slaves who attended to the details of the collection. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="familias_73_17">: see note on Rosc. Am., <ref n="pl15.3">p. 15, l. 3</ref>. The Roman slaves were not merely rude Gauls and Thracians, but educated Greeks and Asiatics. The latter served in noble families as secretaries, stewards, and tutors, and would naturally be employed by the great tax-collecting corporations as agents and servants. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="saltibus_73_18"> ,<gloss>mountain pastures</gloss>. Here again three classes of revenue are alluded to: <foreign xml:lang="lat">scriptura</foreign> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">in saltibus</foreign> ),<foreign xml:lang="lat">decumae</foreign> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">in agris</foreign>),<foreign xml:lang="lat">portoria</foreign> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">in portubus</foreign>). Observe the art with which Cicero constantly repeats, in different order and different terms, the same detailed description of the revenues, in order to keep this important point before the minds of his hearers. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="custodiis_73_19"> ,<gloss> coast-guards</gloss>, stationed to prevent smuggling, at the custom-houses and toll-houses. <pb n="338"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="posse_73_20"> ,<gloss> can</gloss>: for construction of posse, see § <ref n="AG 516"> 516</ref>, d, 584, b (307, d); G. 248, R.; H.-B. 472, d; the <foreign xml:lang="lat">protasis</foreign> is <foreign xml:lang="lat"> nisi</foreign> . . .<foreign xml:lang="lat"> conservantis</foreign> (fut. perf.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ac_ne_74_1">, etc., <gloss>nor must you neglect this point either</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_dicturus_74_2">: see above, sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 6">6</ref>, where the divisions of the subject are specified. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_pertinet_74_3"> ,<gloss>which bears upon</gloss>, etc. The antecedent is </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illud_74_3"> . </gloss>

Two classes are mentioned: (1) the <foreign xml:lang="lat">publicani</foreign> or tax-farmers, and (2) other citizens who have money invested in Asia (sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 18"> 18</ref> ). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="rationes_74_6"> ,<gloss>business enterprises</gloss>;</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="copias_74_6"> ,<gloss> fortunes</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_provinciam_74_6">, i.e. the farming of the revenues there. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ipsorum_se_74_7"> ,<gloss>for their own sake</gloss> (i.e. apart from all question of the safety of the revenues). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nervos_74_8">: the same figure is seen in our phrase "the sinews of war." </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eum_ordinem_74_9">, i.e. the <foreign xml:lang="lat">equites</foreign> these not only farmed the taxes, but they were, in general, the capitalists and bankers of Rome. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ex_ordinibus_74_11"> refers to men of humbler rank who were <gloss>carrying on business</gloss> in Asia, as well as to Senators who had money <gloss> invested</gloss> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">conlocatas</foreign>) there. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eorum_74_13"> (redundant) limits <foreign xml:lang="lat">partim</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="humanitatis_vestrae_74_14">: §<ref n="AG 343">343</ref>, c (214, d); B. 198,3; G. 366; H. 439, 3 (401, N.2); H.-B. 340; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sapientiae_74_14"> is in the same construction. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="etenim_primum_74_17"> introduces the first reason why the losses of private citizens are a matter of public concern; the second reason is introduced by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="deinde_quod_74_17"> (sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 19">19</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illud_refert_74_17">, etc., <gloss>it is of slight consequence that we can afterwards win back in victory</gloss>: §<ref n="AG 417">417</ref> (252, a); B. 211, 3, a; G. 379, 380; H. 449, 3 (408, iii); H.-B. 427, 2, a. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="publica_vectigalia_74_18">, or may be taken absolutely, omitting the doubtful word <foreign xml:lang="lat">vectigalia</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="his_74_18">, i.e. the <foreign xml:lang="lat">publicani</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="amissis_74_18"> ,<gloss> lost</gloss>, i.e. as bidders for the revenues. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="redimendi_74_19"> ,<gloss>contracting for</gloss> the revenues. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="deinde_74_21">: introducing another important point; general credit will invariably suffer when a large class of moneyed men are ruined. The student should remember that Rome was a great commercial <foreign xml:lang="lat">centre</foreign> like London to-day. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="initio_belli_74_22">, i.e. in the First Mithridatic War. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="memoria_74_23">, loc. abl.: § <ref n="AG 429">429</ref>, 3 (254, a); G. 389; H. 485, I (425, I, 2); H.-B. 436, b. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_amiserant_74_23"> ,<gloss>when</gloss> (as you remember), etc.: § <ref n="AG 545">545</ref>(325, a); cf. B. 288, I, a; G. 580; H. 601 (521, ii, I); H-B. 550 and a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="solutione_concidisse_74_24"> (brief description of a financial panic), <gloss>when payment was embarrassed, credit fell</gloss>. similar panics in recent times may help us conceive the political importance of commerce in antiquity. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_possunt_74_25">: translate (to preserve the emphasis), <gloss>for it is impossible that</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_trahant_74_26"> (clause of result), <gloss>without dragging</gloss> (lit. <gloss>so as not to drag</gloss>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="prohibete_74_27">: for the two senses of this verb, see Vocab. (Cf. also <foreign xml:lang="lat">defendo</foreign>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="id_74_28">: §<ref n="AG 362">362</ref>, a (225, a); H.-B. 364, 3 and 4. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ratio_pecuniarum_74_29"> ,<gloss>financial system</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="versatur_74_30"> ,<gloss> centres</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pecunus_74_30"> ,<gloss> finances</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illa_74_31">, i.e. those in Asia; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="haec_74_31">, i.e. at Rome. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="num_sit_74_32"> ,<gloss>whether you ought to hesitate</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dubitandum_sit_74_32">, impersonal. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="incumbere_75_1">: the usual constr. after <foreign xml:lang="lat">non dubito</foreign> in this sense; § <ref n="AG 588">588</ref>, a, N.2 (332, g, N.2); B. 298, b; G. 555, R3; H. 596, I (505, i, 4); cf. H.-B. 586. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fortunae_75_2">, etc.: with this chapter Cicero closes the discussion "<foreign xml:lang="lat">de genere</foreign> <foreign xml:lang="lat">belli</foreign>." There is no anticlimax, for the stability of the whole Roman financial system was of course more important than either the safety of the allies or the revenues of a single province. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="potest_75_5"> (emphatic position), etc., <gloss>it <emph rend="capitalize">may</emph> be said</gloss>, i.e. in answer to the preceding arguments: of course, in order to justify the wisdom of so exceptional a measure as the Manilian Law, it was necessary to show that the war was of sufficient gravity to require the appointment of Pompey. Observe the skillful transition from the <foreign xml:lang="lat"> genus</foreign> of the war to its <foreign xml:lang="lat"> magnitudo</foreign> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="belli_genus_75_5">, i.e. <gloss>the war, in its character</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="elaborandum_est_75_7">: use the personal construction in translating. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ornatas_75_12"> ,<gloss> equipped</gloss>;</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="instructas_75_12"> ,<gloss> organised</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="obsessam_75_14"> ,<gloss> invested</gloss>;</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="oppugnatam_75_14"> ,<gloss> attacked</gloss> (by the active operations of siege): the verb <gloss> besiege</gloss> includes both ideas. This was <date when="-0074">B.C. 74</date>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ducibus_Sertorianis_75_18">: abl. abs. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_Italiam_75_18">: a fleet which Mithridates had despatched for Italy with a contingent furnished by Sertorius, had been defeated by Lucullus near the island of Lemnos. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="studio_75_18"> ,<gloss> zeal</gloss> (for one party); </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="odio_75_18"> ,<gloss> hate</gloss> (for the other). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="proehis_75_20">: §<ref n="AG 424">424</ref>, d (259, a); cf. B. 230, 2; G. 394, a.; Cf. H-B. 439, a, 3. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Pontum_75_21">, i.e. the Euxine Sea. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ex_aditu_75_22"> ,<gloss>at every approach</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Sinopen_75_23">, Amisum: towns on the north coast of Asia Minor. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aditu_75_25"> ,<gloss> approach</gloss>;</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adventu_75_25"> ,<gloss> arrival</gloss>. The fact is, that both Sinope and Amisus had made a very stubborn resistance, which the orator chooses to ignore. A certain vagueness in Cicero's whole account in this and the following chapter is doubtless due to a wish to spare Lucullus. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="alios_reges_75_26">: his son Machares, king of Bosporus, and his son-in-law Tigranes, king of Armenia. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="salvis_75_28">, i.e. without harming the allies </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="integris_75_28">, i.e. without impairing the revenues. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ita_75_29"> ,<gloss>of such a kind</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="a_nullo_75_30">, etc.: thus Cicero's praise of Lucullus has a definite place in the argument. It is important for him to show that this law can be advocated by one who fully appreciates the merits of Lucullus. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="requiretur_76_1"> ,<gloss>the question will be asked</gloss> (emph.) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="primum_76_4">: the corresponding particles ("secondly," etc.) are omitted; the next point begins at sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 23">23</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ponto_76_5">: the old kingdom of Colchis, the scene of Jason's adventures in winning the Golden Fleece, was on the eastern shore of the Euxine and formed a part of Mithridates' kingdom of Pontus. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Medea_76_5">: see Fig. 22 (from a wall-painting). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="persequeretur_76_7"> ,<gloss>was likely to fallow</gloss>. This is a subord. clause in indir. disc.; but, even if the story were being told in din disc. (without <foreign xml:lang="lat">praedicant</foreign>), we should still have <foreign xml:lang="lat">persequeretur</foreign> on the principle of informal indir. disc., expressing the thought of Medea: § <ref n="AG 592">592</ref>, 3 (341, d), B. 323; G. 628; H. 649, I (528 I) H.-B. 509; this is shown by the use of <foreign xml:lang="lat">se</foreign> (not <foreign xml:lang="lat">eam</foreign>) in l.6. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="conlectio_dispersa_76_7"> ,<gloss>the scattered gathering</gloss>, the phrase vividly expresses the idea of his wandering about to pick them up. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vim_auri_76_9">, etc.: the immense treasures which Mithridates had accumulated in his several fortresses came into the hands of Lucullus, —not money simply, but works of art, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illum_76_14"> ,</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hos_76_14">, denote distance and nearness of <gloss>time</gloss>. Render in the pass. to keep the emphasis, <gloss>the one was detained by</gloss>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="erat_enim_76_19">, etc.: explaining the reason why these nations displayed hostility, though the Romans had no designs on them. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eis_nationibus_76_20">, i.e. those near Armenia. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fani_76_23">: "the temple of the Persian Nanaea, or Anaitis, in Elymais, or the modern Luristan [that part of Susiana nearest to the Euphrates], the most celebrated and the richest shrine in the whole region of the Euphrates." Such a rumor would at once fire the population of the whole East. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="urbem_76_27">: Tigranocerta, the new capital of Tigranes, situated in the southwest part of his kingdom, near the river Tigris. This city was destroyed by Lucullus. </gloss>

After all his successes, Lucullus had made somewhat the same mistake as Napoleon in his Russian expedition, and had found himself in an awkward situation, far from his base of operations, and in the midst of infuriated enemies. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hic_76_30"> ,<gloss>on this point</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="extremum_76_30"> ,<gloss>the climax</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_quaereretur_76_31">, subst. clause of result: § <ref n="AG 567">567</ref>(332, head-note); B. 297,3; G. 553,4; H. 571,2 (501, i, 2); cf. H-B. 521, 3, a. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="opes_misericordiam_77_6">: a short expression for <gloss>win them over to pity and call out their resources.</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_videatur_77_7">, a result-clause following </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eo_77_11"> following </gloss>

The last mentioned, that with the pirates (<foreign xml:lang="lat">bellum navale</foreign>), is of course specially dwelt on (sects. <ref n="Cic. Man. 31">31</ref>-<ref n="Cic. Man. 35"> 35</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="neque_illae_79_21">: Cicero does not mention what the other good qualities are till sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 36">36</ref>. By an oratorical device he begins as if he did not mean to talk about the ordinary <foreign xml:lang="lat">virtutes</foreign> recognized as necessary for a general, but intended to speak of certain others, equally necessary but perhaps less common (incorruptibility, etc.), for which Pompey was eminent. But he goes on at once to emphasize the possession of the commonly recognized soldierly qualities by Pompey, as if he had forgotten his point in his enthusiasm. Then, with sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 36">36</ref>, he suddenly pulls himself up, as from a digression, and returns to consider the good qualities he had, as he says, "begun to enumerate." By this method, not only is an air of spontaneity given to the praise of Pompey (as if the orator were carried away by his theme; cf. sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 3">3</ref>), but the special and rare virtues on which he wishes to lay stress are much emphasized by being, as it were, brought in twice, — a second time when the orator seems in danger of forgetting them (sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 36">36</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Cretensibus_83_13">: Quintus Metellus, the proconsul (the friend of Verres), had reduced Crete nearly to submission, deriving from this his <foreign xml:lang="lat">cognomen</foreign> Creticus. Civil war nearly broke out between the two commanders in consequence. Pompey, however, who had his hands full in Asia, withdrew from the field and left the honors to his rival.</gloss>
