<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="amoenitas_85_2">: used of objects of sight, beauty of scenery, etc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="labor_85_3"> ,<gloss> toil, </gloss>always with the sense of effort and fatigue. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ceteri_85">, as Verres, for instance (see "The Plunder of Syracuse," pp.48 ff.). <pb n="349"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="visenda_85_6">: the passion for travel and sight-seeing was as common among the ancients as in modern times (of. "The Plunder of Syracuse," <ref n="pl52.9">p. 52, l. 9</ref>; p.57, ll. 5-7). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fuisse_85_9">: of. <foreign xml:lang="lat">fuit</foreign> on <ref n="pl81.7">p. 81, l. 7</ref> (and note). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hac_continentia_85_9">, i.e. such as his. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jam_videbatur_85_10"> ,<gloss>was now getting to seem</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nunc_85_11">: notice the emphatic repetition (<foreign xml:lang="lat">anaphora</foreign> ). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="servire_imperare_85_14">: a rhetorical exaggeration for preferring the condition of subject allies to nominal independence. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="consiho_86_1">, etc.: of. p.83,ll.26, 27. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ipso_86_2"> ,<gloss>of itself</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hoc_loco_86_3">, i.e. the Rostra. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fidem_vero_86_4">, etc.: render <gloss>and as to his good faith</gloss>, etc., changing the construction so as to keep the emphasis. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quam_86_5">, etc.: render <gloss>when the <emph>enemy</emph> esteemed it</gloss>, etc. (contrasting <foreign xml:lang="lat">hostes</foreign> with <foreign xml:lang="lat">socios</foreign>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pugnantes_86_7"> ,<gloss>in battle</gloss>;</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="victi_86_7"> ,<gloss>in defeat</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_ament_86_17">: clause of result, dependent on <foreign xml:lang="lat">commoveri</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="judicia_86_22"> ,<gloss>expressions of opinion</gloss> (i.e. by conferring offices on him) ; of. what Cicero says of himself in sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 2">2</ref> (p. 68, ll.12, 13). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ullam_usquam_86_23">: see note on sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 27">27</ref> (<ref n="pl78.25">p. 78, l. 25</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illius_diei_86_24">: that of the passage of the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Lex Gabinia</foreign>, which conferred upon Pompey the command against the pirates. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="commune_86_26">: as being against pirates, enemies of all mankind. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aliorum_exemplis_86_28">: it is not necessary to cite examples of other generals; Pompey's own history furnishes instances enough. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_die_86_31">, on <gloss>the day on which be</gloss>, etc.: the relatives, admissible in Latin, cannot be literally reproduced in English. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="potuisset_87_3">: §<ref n="AG 517">517</ref>, c, N.1 (308, c, N.1) B. 304, 3, a, N.; G. 597, R.3, b; of. 14.511, I, N.1 (583); H-B. 582,3,0. The <foreign xml:lang="lat">protasis</foreign> is implied in <foreign xml:lang="lat">in summa ubertate</foreign>, etc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="proelio_87_4">: the defeat of Triarius (see sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 25">25</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="provincia_87_6">, i.e. Asia. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_regiones_87_8">, i.e. only <gloss>into the neighborhood</gloss>, for Pompey's authority did not extend to the seat of war; this force is given by the preposition <foreign xml:lang="lat">ad</foreign>:<foreign xml:lang="lat">in</foreign> would mean <gloss>into</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="perfecturus_sit_87_12">: §<ref n="AG 575">575</ref>, a (334, a); cf. B. 269, 3; G. 514, b; H. 649, ii, I (529, ii, 4); 14.-B. 537, d, I. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="perfecerit_87_12">: subj. of characteristic. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illa_res_87_15">: in appos. with the clause <foreign xml:lang="lat">quod . . . dediderunt</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Cretensium_87_18">: towns of the same region or race were often united in leagues or confederacies, chiefly for religious purposes. After the Roman conquest, such <foreign xml:lang="lat">communia</foreign> were sometimes left in existence, and even new ones were organized and allowed to exercise some subordinate political function. The existence of a <foreign xml:lang="lat">commune Cretensium</foreign> is known from inscriptions. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="noster_imperator_87_19">: Q. Metellus (see note on <ref n="pl83.13">p. 83, l. 13</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_eundem_87_22">, i.e. to Pompey. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eum_quem_87_23"> ,<gloss>one who</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ei_quibus_87_24"> ,<gloss>while they</gloss>, etc., i.e. those who were jealous of Pompey's reputation. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="potissimum_87_25"> ,<gloss>rather than to any one else</gloss> (i.e. rather than to Q. Metellus Pius, who also had a command in Spain and who was much older than Pompey). Nothing further is known of this embassy. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hanc_auctoritatem_87_27">: translate, <gloss>as to this prestige</gloss>, though it is really the subject of <foreign xml:lang="lat">valituram esse</foreign>, the whole clause being governed by <foreign xml:lang="lat">existimetis</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="felicitate_87_30">: in this quality is implied a special favor of the gods, which it would be presumptuous to arrogate to one's self (hence <foreign xml:lang="lat">timide</foreign>), although Sulla had done so by assuming the <foreign xml:lang="lat">cognomen</foreign> Felix (see Rosc. Am., sect. <ref n="Cic. S. Rosc. 12">12</ref>,<ref n="pl6.7">p. 6, l. 7</ref>, and note). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Maximo_88_2_3">: Quintus Fabius Maximus, "the shield of Rome"; Marcello: Marcus Claudius Marcellus, "the sword of Rome," both distinguished in the Second Punic War. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Scipioni_88_2_3">: either Africanus the elder, or Aemilianus; from sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 60">60</ref> it might appear to be the latter. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Mario_88_2_3">: Caius Marius, who vanquished Jugurtha, subdued the Cimbri and Teutones, and afterwards (<date when="-0088">B.C. 88</date>) engaged in civil war with Sulla. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="saepius_88_4"> ,<gloss> repeatedly</gloss>: Marius was consul seven times. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fuit_88_5"> (emphatic), <gloss>there really has been</gloss>; §<ref n="AG 598">598</ref>, d (2) (344, d, 2). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hac_moderatione_88_9">: a shorthand expression for <foreign xml:lang="lat">hoc modo moderato</foreign>, in which <foreign xml:lang="lat">moderato</foreign> would refer merely to the result clause <foreign xml:lang="lat">ut videamur</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_ut_88_9"> (not to be confounded with <foreign xml:lang="lat">ut non</foreign>), etc., i.e. not of such a kind as to say, etc., but such, etc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="invisa_88_11">: because presumptuous. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_praedicaturus_88_13">: this affectation of passing a subject over in silence is called <foreign xml:lang="lat">praeteritio</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_88_14"> ,<gloss> how</gloss> (introducing an indir. quest.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_auderet_88_18">: rel. clause of result. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quot_quantas_88_19">, correl. with <foreign xml:lang="lat">tot et tantas</foreign> above. Translate by the single word <gloss>as</gloss>; §<ref n="AG 308"> 308</ref>, h (106, b). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="proprium_perpetuum_88_20"> ,<gloss>secured to him forever</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_88_21">, introducing the general consideration (<foreign xml:lang="lat"> communis</foreign> );</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tum_88_21"> (next line), the particular consideration (<foreign xml:lang="lat"> ipsius</foreign>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sit_88_26">: subj. of characteristic. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quin_conferatis_88_28">: §<ref n="AG 558">558</ref>, a, N.2 (332, g, N.2); B. 298, b; G. 555, 2, R.3 H. 596, I (505, I); Cf. H-B. 502, 3, b. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="erat_deligendus_88_32">: §<ref n="AG 517">517</ref>, c (308, c); B. 304, 3, a; G. 597, R2; 11.582 (51 I, 2); H-B. 582, 3, a, N.1. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nunc_89_1"> ,<gloss>as things stand</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adsit_89_3"> ,</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="habeat_89_3"> ,</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="possit_89_3">: result clauses in appos. with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="opportunitas_89_3"> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eis_habent_89_3">, i.e. Lucullus, Glabrio, and Marcius Rex, who were still in command of Roman armies in Asia. For mood of <foreign xml:lang="lat">habent</foreign>, see § <ref n="AG 593">593</ref>, a, N.1 (342, a, N.); G. 629, R.; H. 652, I (529, ii, N.1, 2). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cur_committamus_89_4">: observe the different mood in the preceding question </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_exspectamus_89_4"> ? </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="at_enim_89_8"> (objection), <gloss>but, you will say</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adfectus_89_9"> =<gloss> enjoying</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Catulus_89_9">: Quintus Lutatius Catulus, at this time the leader of the senatorial party; an estimable man and an experienced statesman, but no soldier. The <foreign xml:lang="lat">beneficia amplissima</foreign> are the successive offices that had been conferred upon him. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Hortensius_89_111">: the leading lawyer of the time (see oration against Verres). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ratione_89_111"> ,<gloss> view</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="auctoritates_contrarias_89_14">: of course there were men of influence on the side of the Manilian Law as well as opposed to it; Cicero brings forward the names of several in sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 68">68</ref>, below. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ipsa_ratione_89_15">: this appeal from theoretical objections (as Cicero thinks them) to experience (i.e. in the Piratic War) would, of course, be very effective in a public assembly, for theoretical considerations weigh little with such bodies in comparison with facts. Cicero makes it doubly effective by pointing out that his opponents agree with his premises as to the necessity and magnitude of the war and the eminent ability of Pompey as a general, but that they avoid, on these merely technical grounds, what seems to him the obvious conclusion: viz. that Pompey should be appointed. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tribuenda_sint_89_20">: condition with nothing implied (in dir. disc., <foreign xml:lang="lat">sunt</foreign>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pro_89_23">, in <gloss>accordance with</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_senatu_89_24">: laws did not require any ratification by the Senate. The expression of opinion by Hortensius must therefore have been in an informal discussion, after the promulgation of the law (i.e. its announcement as a proposed bill). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Gabiniurh_89_25">: see Introd., p.67 of text. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="promulgasset_89_27">: a bill intended to be brought before either <foreign xml:lang="lat"> comitia</foreign> was regularly announced to the Senate and posted in the city two Roman weeks (at least 17 days) before it could be voted on. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ex_loco_89_27">, i.e. in the public discussion of the law, before the vote, in the <foreign xml:lang="lat">contio</foreign> (see sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 1"> 1</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hanc_89_31">, i.e. which we have now. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="an_90_1"> implies a strong negative; § <ref n="AG 335">335</ref>, b (211, b); B. 162,4, a; G 457, 1; H. 380, 3 (353, N.4); H.-B. 236. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="legati_90_2">, etc.: observe that Cicero seizes the opportunity to recall briefly to the minds of his hearers certain important facts which he has already dwelt on in greater detail (in sects. <ref n="Cic. Man. 31"> 31</ref>-<ref n="Cic. Man. 33">33</ref> ). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ex_omnibus_90_3">, etc.: trans. from <gloss>communication</gloss> (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="commeato_90_3">, really abl. of specification) <gloss>with all the provinces</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="neque_jam_90_3"> (1.6), <gloss>no longer</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Atheniensium_90_8">: the Athenian empire of the sea, in the fifth century B.C., resulted from the great victories in the Persian War. <pb n="353"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Karthaginiensium_90_9">: the maritime power of Carthage was at its height in the third century B.C. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Rhodiorum_90_10">: the city of Rhodes was the chief naval power of the Mediterranean during the last three centuries before Christ: its power was broken <date when="-0042">B.C. 42</date>, at its capture by Cassius. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="permanserit_90_17">: subj. of characteristic. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Antiochum_90_19">: Antiochus the Great, king of Syria, defeated at Magnesia, <date when="-0190">B.C. 190</date>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Persen_90_20">: Perses or Perseus, the last king of Macedonia, defeated at Pydna, <date when="-0168">B.C. 168</date>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Karthaginiensis_90_20">: Carthage was mistress of the sea at the time when her wars with Rome began; but in the First Punic War she was beaten at her own weapons. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ei_90_22"> repeats </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nos_90_22"> :<gloss> we</gloss>, i.e. that nation. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Delos_91_1">: a very small island in the Aegean Sea, sacred as the birthplace of Apollo and Artemis. It had an excellent harbor, and this, added to its peculiar sanctity, gave it high importance. It had at all times a flourishing commerce and in the time of Cicero was the great slave market of the world, 10,000 slaves being sometimes sold there in a single day. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eidem_91_3"> repeats </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nos_91_3"> (1.23, above). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Appia_via_91_5">: the principal highway of Italy, running from Rome to Capua, and thence to Brundisium (see map of Italy, p.1). It was begun by Appius Claudius Caecus, in his censorship, <date when="-0312">B.C. 312</date> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jam_91_5"> ,<gloss>at length</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pudebat_magistratus_91_6"> (acc. pl.): no special case is referred to, but it is implied that any magistrate ought to have felt shame, seeing that the beaks of ships, <foreign xml:lang="lat">rostra</foreign>, were naval trophies. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_91_7">: concessive. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dolori_91_12">: we should be likely to use a more general word, like <gloss>feelings</gloss>, which would be defined by the context. Such differences between two languages in the expression of thought are constantly found. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aliquando_91_5"> ,<gloss>at last</gloss> (cf. Cat. 2, sect. 1, l. 1). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="obtrectatum_esse_91_17">: the subject of <foreign xml:lang="lat">obtrectatum esse</foreign> is the wish of the opponents to defeat the proposed measure (the appointment of Gabinius as lieutenant); as this wish, if successful, would be (like the affirmative measure) a <gloss> determination</gloss>, it is expressed by a purpose clause, <foreign xml:lang="lat">ne legaretur</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adhuc_91_17">: this opposition began in connection with the Gabinian Law and is still continued in connection with the Manilian. <pb n="355"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_liceat_92_16">, i.e. how far they can safely go. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="considerabunt_92_16">, i.e. before they set themselves against the manifest will of the people. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="socius_92_18">: not as <foreign xml:lang="lat">legatus</foreign>, but simply as <gloss> partner</gloss> in his former honor and credit. This association of Gabinius with Pompey is used as an argument for giving him the office of <foreign xml:lang="lat"> legatus</foreign> now.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="auctoritate_sententia_92_22"> , i.e. the weight which one must attach to the opinion of so great a man as Catulus (a kind of hendiadys). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_quaereret_92_23">: cf. <foreign xml:lang="lat">cum</foreign> . . .<foreign xml:lang="lat">dixistis</foreign>, just below; § <ref n="AG 546">546</ref>, N.3 (323); G. 579; H. 600, ii, I (521; ii, 2); H-B. 524 and c. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_poneretis_92_24">: fut. <foreign xml:lang="lat">protasis</foreign>; the apod. is the compound sentence <foreign xml:lang="lat">si</foreign> . . .<foreign xml:lang="lat">factum esset, in quo spem essetis habituri</foreign>, which itself consists of a fut. prot. and apod.; § <ref n="AG 523"> 523</ref> (31r, d); H-B. 582, 4. The tenses depend for their sequence on the perf. <foreign xml:lang="lat">cepit</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_esset_92_24"> ,<gloss>if anything should happen to him</gloss> (a common euphemism then as now). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="essetis_habituri_92_25">: indir. quest.; for use of periphrastic form, see § <ref n="AG 575"> 575</ref>, a (334, a); B. 269,3; G. 515; H. 649, ii, I (529 , ii,4); H-B. 537, d, I. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quominus_magis_92_31">: §<ref n="AG 414">414</ref>,a(250,R.); B.223; G.403; H. 479 (423); H.-B. 424. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="at_enim_92_33">: see first note on sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 51">51</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="exempla_93_1">, precedents; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="instituta_93_1">, established customs. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_dicam_93_1">, etc.: an excellent specimen of the rhetorical device known as <foreign xml:lang="lat">praeteritio</foreign> (cf. note on <ref n="pl88.13">p. 88, l. 13</ref>, above). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="paruisse_93_3"> ,</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adcommodasse_93_3">, i.e. they disregarded precedents in great emergencies. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="temporum_casus_93_3"> ,<foreign xml:lang="lat">consiliorum</foreign> on <foreign xml:lang="lat">rationes</foreign> (chiastic order). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ab_imperatore_93_5">: Scipio Africanus the younger (Aemilianus), who captured Carthage (<date when="-0146">B.C. 146</date>) and Numantia (<date when="-0133">B.C. 133</date>). In his time there had been a law that no person should be consul twice in successlon. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_poneretur_93_9">: clause of purpose with <foreign xml:lang="lat">visum est</foreign> (here a verb of decreeing). <pb n="356"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="C_Mario_93_10">: Marius was chosen consul five years in succession, to carry on the wars here referred to. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="privatum_93_15">, i.e. not a magistrate. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="a_gradu_93_18">: no one could legally enter the Senate until after holding the quaestorship, the minimum age for which was thirty at least, and regularly thirty-six, while Pompey was at the time referred to (<date when="-0082">B.C. 82</date>) only twenty-three. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_provincia_93_20">, i.e. Africa. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fuit_93_21">: translate, <gloss>he showed</gloss>, etc. (in order to render the abls. of quality, which come in a way foreign to our idiom). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="victorem_93_23"> ,<gloss> victorious</gloss> (pred. adj.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="exercitum_deportavit_93_23">: this was one of the essential conditions of a triumph. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="equitem_93_24">, i.e. not a member of the Senate, having never held a magistracy. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="triumphare_93_24">: the honor of a triumph was restricted to commanders who possessed the imperium by virtue of holding a regular magistracy. Until he was elected consul for the year <date when="-0070">B.C. 70</date>, Pompey had never had the imperium except by special appointment from the Senate; both his triumphs, therefore, <date when="-0080">B.C. 80</date> and 71, were contrary to precedent. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="duo_consules_93_27">: Mamercus Lepidus and Decimus Brutus, <date when="-0077">B.C. 77</date>. Instead of either of these being sent to Spain as proconsul the next year, against Sertorius Pompey, though a simple <foreign xml:lang="lat">eques</foreign>, was designated for that service. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pro_consule_93_29">: when it was desired to retain the services of a magistrate after his term of office had expired, his imperium was extended (<foreign xml:lang="lat">prorogatum</foreign>) by the Senate, and was held by him <foreign xml:lang="lat">pro consule</foreign> or <foreign xml:lang="lat">pro praetore</foreign>, that is, as having the power of a consul or <foreign xml:lang="lat">praetor</foreign> while no longer actually a magistrate. It was not strictly legal to appoint a private citizen in such a capacity; but sometimes,— as in Pompey's case, —this was done. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quidem_93_29">, by the way. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_nemo_93_30">, a man or two. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Philippus_93_31">, a prominent member of the aristocracy (consul, <date when="-0091">B.C. 91</date>), distinguished for his wit; a man of liberal temper, but a vehement partisan. <pb n="357"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pro_consulibus_93_32"> ,<gloss>in place of both consuls</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mittere_93_33">: for <foreign xml:lang="lat">mitto</foreign> of the dir. disc. Philippus seems to have put his <foreign xml:lang="fra">bon mot</foreign> into the regular form of a <foreign xml:lang="lat"> sententia</foreign>, or formal expression of opinion in the Senate, using the simple present tense, with the qualifying </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mea_sententia_93_33">; §<ref n="AG 467">467</ref>(276,b); B. 259,2; G.227,N.2; H. 530(467, iii, 6); H.-B. 484. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_fieret_94_2">: subst. clause of result after the analogy of the subj. with verbs of happening; § <ref n="AG 571">571</ref>,c (332,f); G. 553,4; H. 571, I (501, i). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ex_consulto_94_2">: another irregularity, for the <foreign xml:lang="lat">comitia</foreign> were the law-making body and therefore of course had the sole power of exempting from the laws. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="legibus_solutus_94_2"> ,<gloss>exempted from the operation of the laws</gloss>, i.e. those limiting the age of magistrates (<foreign xml:lang="lat">leges annales</foreign>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="magistratum_94_3">: the legal age of a consul was not below forty-three, and that of a <foreign xml:lang="lat">praetor</foreign> not below forty. Pompey, however, was elected consul (<date when="-0070">B.C. 70</date>) at the age of thirty-six, which was the regular age for the quaestorship. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="iterum_94_4">: Pompey celebrated his second triumph Dec.31, <date when="-0071">B.C. 71</date>, and the next day entered upon the consulship. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_94_5"> ,<gloss>in the case of</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="atque_haec_94_8">, etc., <gloss>and all these many precedents, so weighty and so new, have been established in the case of this single man</gloss> (Pompey), <gloss>and have originated, too, in measures promoted by Q. Catulus and the other</gloss>, etc. Lit. <gloss>all these many examples (i.e. acts establishing precedents) have come upon this same man (Pompey) [proceeding] from the [senatorial] approval of Q. Catulus</gloss>, etc. The Latin tends to compress two or more assertions into a single clause, where in English it is more natural to use separate clauses. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="amplissimorum_94_10">: a regular epithet for dignitaries. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="auctoritate_94_10">, i.e. since they were then prominent members of the Senate. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="comprobatam_94_13">, i.e. the people, in electing Pompey consul, had only followed the example of the Senate in conferring these repeated honors. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="judicium_94_14"> ,<gloss>formal decision</gloss>, expressed by passing the Gabinian Law. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vel_94_16"> ,<gloss> even</gloss> .<pb n="358"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="delegistis_94_18">: not literally correct. The Gabinian Law merely prescribed that <gloss>an ex-consul</gloss> should receive this command: the Senate selected the man. In fact, however, it was a law made for Pompey, and the Senate would not have ventured to appoint anybody else. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sin_94_21">: the <foreign xml:lang="lat">protasis</foreign> extends to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="attulistis_94_21">, the connective being omitted. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="plus_vidistis_94_21"> ,<gloss>had a keener insight in affairs of state</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aliquando_94_23">: cf. <ref n="pl91.15">p. 91, l. 15</ref>, and note. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="isti_94_23">: this pron., since it is often used of an opponent in a suit, here at once suggests the opposition now existing between Cicero and Catulus. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="auctoritati_94_24">: §<ref n="AG 372">372</ref> (230); B. 256, 3; G. 217; H. 426, 1,518, I (385, i, 465, I); H.-B. 364, 2. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Asiatico_regio_94_26">: the two adjectives enhance the impression of the difficulty of the war by emphasizing its distance and the dignity of the enemy. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="versari_94_30"> ,<gloss>conduct himself</gloss> (see Vocab.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_sunt_95_1"> ,<gloss>when they are</gloss> (lit. <foreign xml:lang="lat">if there are</foreign> any). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pudore_95_1"> (abl. of specification), <gloss>respect for others</gloss>;</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="temperantia_95_1"> ,<gloss> self-restraint</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="requiruntur_95_9"> ,<gloss>are in demand</gloss>, i.e. pretexts of war are sought, with cities that we hardly know of; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="inferatur_95_9"> ,<gloss>may be fastened</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="libenter_95_11">, etc., <gloss>I should be glad to argue this face to face</gloss>, etc.; § <ref n="AG 521">521</ref>, a (310, a); B. 305, 1; G. 600, 1; H. 575, 9 (507, N.7); H.-B. 578, 6. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hostium_simulatione_95_15"> ,<gloss>under a pretence of</gloss> [the existence of] <gloss> enemies</gloss>: notice the chiastic order. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="animos_spiritus_95_17"> ,<gloss>pride and insolence</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="conlatis_signis_95_19">, i.e. an actual warfare. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nisi_idem_95_20"> ,<gloss>unless he shall also be one</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="idoneus_mittatur_95_24">: see note on <foreign xml:lang="lat">impetret</foreign>,<ref n="pl91.21">p. 91, l. 21</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pacatam_95_25">, etc.: in the forcible extension of the Roman Empire, a province was spoken of as <foreign xml:lang="lat">pacata</foreign> when actual resistance had ceased on the part of the conquered. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quae_sit_95_25">, subj. of characteristic; for tense, see § <ref n="AG 435">435</ref>, c (287, c); B. 268, 7; G. 513; H. 550 (495, vi); cf. H.-B. 481. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praetores_95_29">, i.e. <gloss> propraetors</gloss>: for, after the time of Sulla, the praetors regularly remained at Rome during their term of office. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="publica_95_30">, i.e. that allowed them for the support of their fleets and armies. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jacturis_95_33"> ,<gloss> expenses</gloss>, in securing their election. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="condicionibus_95_34"> ,<gloss>corrupt bargains</gloss>, with creditors, etc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quasi_vldeamus_96_1">: §<ref n="AG 524">524</ref>(312); B.307,2;G.602; H. 534 and 2 (513, ii, and N.1); H.-B. 504, 3. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dubitare_quin_96_3"> ,<gloss> hesitate</gloss>. The usual construction in this sense would be with the infin.; § <ref n="AG 558">558</ref>, a, N.1 (332,g, N.2); B. 298, b; G. 555, R3; 14.596, I (505, i); cf. H.-B. 502, 3, b, 586. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="auctoritatibus_96_7">, i.e. the opinions of influential men (cf. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="auctor_96_7"> in the next line). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="est_auctor_96_8"> ,<gloss>you have as authority</gloss>. P. Servilius (Vatia Isauricus) was one of the most reputable men of the time. He held the proconsulship of Cilicia, <date from="-0078" to="-0075"> <date when="-0078">B.C. 78</date>-75</date>, in which he gained great successes over the pirates. It was probably his intimate knowledge of the region and the kind of warfare, that led him to support this vigorous measure. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="debeat_96_11">: for tense, see § <ref n="AG 435">435</ref>, a (287, a); Cf. B. 268, 1; H-B. 481. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Curio_96_11">: see note on Impeachment of Verres, sect. 18, <ref n="pl34.29">p. 34, l. 29</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Lentulus_96_13">: Cn. Cornelius Lentulus Clodianus, cos. <date when="-0072">B.C. 72</date>; not to be confounded with Lentulus Sura, ens. <date when="-0071">B.C. 71</date>, the accomplice of Catiline. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Cassius_96_15">: for the character of this family, see note on Verr. 1, sect. <ref n="Cic. Ver. 1.1.30">30</ref>,<ref n="pl39.3">p. 39, l. 3</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="auctore_Romano_96_21">: the Roman people has already shown its opinion of Pompey by passing the Gabinian Law; hence Manilius has the <foreign xml:lang="lat"> auctoritas</foreign> of the whole people behind him, as opposed to the <foreign xml:lang="lat"> auctoritas</foreign> of a few aristocrats like Hortensius and Catulus (cf. sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 63">63</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="neve_96_22"> ,<gloss>and not</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="iterum_96_25">: alluding to the former unanimity of the people in passing the Gabinian Law. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="de_facultate_96_27"> ,<gloss>the cause itself or the power of carrying it through</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dubitemus_96_27">: §<ref n="AG 535">535</ref>, a (320, a); B. 283, 2; G. 631, a.2; F 591, 1(503, i); Cf. H.-B. 513, 2. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="potestate_praetoria_96_29"> ,<gloss>official influence as <foreign xml:lang="lat">praetor</foreign> </gloss>; more official than <foreign xml:lang="lat">auctoritate</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="defero_97_1"> ,<gloss>put at your service</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="templo_97_2">, i.e. the <foreign xml:lang="lat">rostra</foreign>. The term <foreign xml:lang="lat"> templum</foreign> was applied to any place consecrated by regular auspices (<foreign xml:lang="lat">augurato</foreign>). As the public assembly was held <foreign xml:lang="lat">augurato</foreign>, the place of holding it was a consecrated one. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_adeunt_97_3"> ,<gloss>are engaged in public affairs</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="neque_quo_97_4"> ,<gloss>nor because</gloss>; §<ref n="AG 540">540</ref>, N.1 (321., a.): B. 286, I, b; G. 541, a.2; H. 588, ii, 2 (516, ii, 2); H-B. 535, 2, b. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="honoribus_97_7">: the term <gloss>honor</gloss> is regularly applied to honors conferred by the people, i.e. <gloss>public offices</gloss>. These he proposes to earn, not by the arts of a politician, but by fidelity in his profession as an advocate. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pericula_97_7"> relates to the <foreign xml:lang="lat">simultates</foreign> in the next section. It was not possible for him to espouse this democratic measure so earnestly without incurring coolness, at least on the part of the aristocracy. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_97_7"> ,<gloss>so far as a <emph rend="capitalize">man</emph> </gloss>, etc. (cf. our <q rend="double">humanly speaking</q> <q rend="double">the Lord willing,</q> and the like.) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ab_uno_97_9">, i.e. he expects no reward in the way of public office from Pompey's influence. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ex_loco_97_9">, i.e. by political activity (cf. sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 1">sect. 1</ref> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tantum_videar_97_13"> ,<gloss>I am so far from seeming:</gloss> §<ref n="AG 571">571</ref>, b (332, d); G. 552, a.1; H. 570, 2 (502, 3); cf. H-B. 521, 3, a, footnote 2, (3). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hoc_honore_97_16">, i.e. the praetorship. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="oportere_97_20"> ,<gloss>I am bound</gloss>:</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="me_97_20"> (l.16) is subject of <foreign xml:lang="lat">praeferre</foreign>, and me <foreign xml:lang="lat">praeferre</foreign> depends on <foreign xml:lang="lat">oportere</foreign>. </gloss>
